title: The Memory Horizon Limit
concept: Vanishing gradients limit a vanilla RNN's memory horizon, preventing it from tracking dependencies over long text spans. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Long-Range Dependency Limits.'

exercises.prompt: What does the abbreviation RNN stand for in machine learning?
exercises.explain: Vanilla Recurrent Neural Networks process data sequentially but struggle to retain information over long sequences.

cards.title: Short Memory Spans
cards.body: RNNs are great at processing sequences, but they have a terrible short-term memory! If you give them a long paragraph, they completely forget how it started. Let's find out why they lose their memory horizon.

cards.title: The Memory Horizon
cards.body: The memory horizon is the maximum distance over which an RNN can successfully link past information to the present. Because of vanishing gradients, vanilla RNNs have a very short memory horizon, quickly forgetting what they read at the start of a long paragraph.

cards.title: The Telephone Game
cards.body: Imagine trying to remember a long sequence of whispers in a game of telephone. As the message travels through 50 people, the original whisper fades away until the last person hears only silence. That is a vanilla RNN trying to pass a memory through many time steps!

cards.title: A Fading Subject
cards.body: Consider this long sentence: "The *dog* that had brown spots, chased the cat, ran through the mud, and barked at the mailman finally fell asleep." \n\nA vanilla RNN might forget the subject was "dog" by the time it reaches the verb "fell asleep", because the memory faded over the many words in between.

cards.title: It's Math, Not Storage!
cards.body: Some believe RNNs lose memory because they run out of storage space. In truth, the model has plenty of space, but the mathematical signal (the gradient) shrinks so small during training that the network physically cannot learn to connect past words to present ones.

cards.title: Visualizing Memory Horizons
cards.body: Comparing how information behaves over long steps in different memory horizons.

right.text: Signals are preserved. The model remembers details from the very beginning.

left.text: Signals shrink rapidly. The start of the sentence is completely forgotten by the end.

exercises.prompt: What happens to gradients in a vanilla RNN as they propagate backward through many steps?

options.text: They shrink closer and closer to zero.
They stay exactly the same size.

exercises.explain: Vanishing gradients make the training signal vanish as it travels back in time, meaning weights associated with early inputs never get updated.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A vanilla RNN is highly effective at tracking context across hundreds of words.
exercises.explain: Vanilla RNNs are notoriously bad at keeping track of context across long paragraphs due to their limited memory horizon.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the term that describes why RNNs lose their long-term memory.
exercises.sentence: The memory horizon is limited because of the vanishing gradient problem.
exercises.explain: The shrinking of training signals is called the vanishing gradient problem, which limits how far back an RNN can remember.

exercises.prompt: We test two RNN models on a long paragraph. Which model displays the classic memory horizon limitation?

options.text: Model A: Accurately links the subject in the 1st sentence to a verb in the 20th sentence.
Model B: Completely forgets the gender of the main character by the end of the page.
Both models perform identically on long-range tasks.

exercises.explain: Model B represents a normal vanilla RNN showing the typical vanishing gradient problem, where early context is lost. Model A is either an LSTM or a model that hasn't lost the gradient.

exercises.prompt: Why exactly does a vanilla RNN lose its memory horizon for words at the very beginning of a long sentence?

options.text: It actively deletes early words to save system memory.
Gradients shrink to zero, preventing weights from updating for early inputs.
The network only processes the last word of any input text.
It runs out of physical RAM on the graphics card.

exercises.explain: With vanishing gradients, the mathematical signals updates shrink to zero, leaving the RNN unable to update its early weights. Thus, it can only 'remember' recent steps. Next up: Long-Range Dependency Limits.
